title: Te Hauora o te Awa: The Health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | Te Hauora o te Awa: Te Hauora o te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the health and wellbeing of our local Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant awa to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore the deep connection between the mauri (life force) of the waterway and the holistic health of our community, drawing on pūrākau (traditional stories) of the taniwha who protects these waters and the historical significance of the stream as a source of kai (food) and wai (water) for local hapū.
learningObjectives: Analyze the relationship between the mauri (life force) of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream and the holistic hauora (wellbeing) of our local community, using a whare tapa whā model.
Collaboratively design and conduct a practical water quality test of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, measuring pH, clarity, and macroinvertebrate presence.
Create a 'Hauora Action Plan' that proposes one specific, evidence-based initiative to improve either the health of the awa or the community's connection to it.

activities.title: He Whakawhiti Kōrero: Introducing our Awa
activities.description: Students are welcomed onto the banks of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. A local kaumātua or representative from Manaaki Te Awa Trust shares the pūrākau (story) of the stream and its taniwha kaitiaki, explaining its traditional role as a source of food, transport, and spiritual wellbeing. Students begin a 'Hauora Journal' to record their initial observations and feelings about the place.

activities.title: Te Whare Tapa Whā o te Awa
activities.description: In small groups, students use the whare tapa whā model to analyze the health of the stream and its connection to community wellbeing. Taha Tinana: Water quality for swimming and food gathering. Taha Hinengaro: The sense of peace and identity the stream provides. Taha Whānau: The stream as a place for community gathering and shared kaitiakitanga. Taha Wairua: The mauri and spiritual significance of the water. Groups present their analyses to the class.

activities.title: He Tātari Wai: Testing the Mauri of the Water
activities.description: A practical, hands-on session where students, guided by an expert from the Hawke's Bay Regional Council, use water testing kits to measure the stream's pH, clarity, and temperature. They will also use kick-nets to collect macroinvertebrates (e.g., kōura, mayfly larvae) and use identification charts to assess the health of the stream ecosystem based on the species found.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi: Designing a Hauora Action Plan
activities.description: Using their collected data and cultural understanding, groups brainstorm a tangible project to enhance the hauora of the stream or the community's relationship with it. Ideas could include: designing and planting a riparian margin with harakeke and native sedges; creating an educational pamphlet for the community; or planning a community clean-up day. They must justify their plan using evidence from their testing and the whare tapa whā framework.

resources: Manaaki Te Awa Trust: Local environmental group and repository of mātauranga Māori specific to our awa.
Hawke's Bay Regional Council: 'Wai Wise' educational water testing kits and macroinvertebrate identification guides.
Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand: Online entries on 'Traditional Māori Storytelling – Pūrākau' and 'Māori Creation Traditions'.
Local kaumātua or kuia from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau and historical knowledge of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream.

tasks: Submit the completed 'Hauora Journal' with observations, water quality data, and macroinvertebrate findings.
Present the group's 'Hauora Action Plan' to the class, explaining how it addresses findings from the whare tapa whā analysis and water quality testing.

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development, A3 - Safety Management; Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments, D1 - Societal Attitudes and Values, D2 - Community Resources, D3 - Rights, Responsibilities, and Laws; D4 - People and the Environment
